metadata.title: The Principal Hierarchy: Why Claude Has Three Bosses.

skin_warnings: B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'LogoOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · The Principal Hierarchy: Why Claude Has Three Bosses.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Three principals hold different trust levels: Anthropic's baked-in rules, operators, and users. Anthropic's training-time rules are the innermost ring and cannot be overridden by anyone. Operators set the system prompt and can restrict defaults but cannot weaponize Claude against users. Users keep a floor: Claude admits when it can't help, won't deny being an AI, and refers emergencies.

artifactLines: Three principals hold different trust levels: Anthropic's baked-in rules, operators, and users.
Anthropic's training-time rules are the innermost ring and cannot be overridden by anyone.
Operators set the system prompt and can restrict defaults but cannot weaponize Claude against users.
Users keep a floor: Claude admits when it can't help, won't deny being an AI, and refers emergencies.

beats.narration_text: Here is why The Principal Hierarchy: Why Claude Has Three Bosses. belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

artifactLines: Three principals hold different trust levels: Anthropic's baked-in rules, operators, and users.
Anthropic's training-time rules are the innermost ring and cannot be overridden by anyone.
Operators set the system prompt and can restrict defaults but cannot weaponize Claude against users.
Users keep a floor: Claude admits when it can't help, won't deny being an AI, and refers emergencies.

beats.narration_text: The load-bearing distinction — an operator can tell Claude not to discuss competitors but can't tell it to deceive users into a bad purchase — is the part I want to pressure-test. Walking the three rings from Anthropic to operator to user, can you (1) give three operator instructions that are allowed and three that cross into weaponizing Claude against users; (2) explain what "the floor belongs to the user" actually guarantees even under restriction; (3) show why the system prompt makes operators accountable rather than being a bug?

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · AI DESIGN · ANTHROPIC RESEARCH
props.segment: The Principal Hierarchy: Why Claude Has Three Bosses.
props.command: The load-bearing distinction — an operator can tell Claude not to discuss competitors but can't tell it to deceive users into a bad purchase — is the part I want to pressure-test. Walking the three rings from Anthropic to operator to user, can you (1) give three operator instructions that are allowed and three that cross into weaponizing Claude against users; (2) explain what "the floor belongs to the user" actually guarantees even under restriction; (3) show why the system prompt makes operators accountable rather than being a bug?

beats.narration_text: Three principals, three trust levels. Anthropic's rules are baked in at training time — they are the innermost ring and cannot be overridden. Operators get the system prompt — they take contractual responsibility for their deployment and can expand or restrict Claude's defaults. Users get the conversation — the outermost ring, highest flexibility, lowest inherent trust.

graphic.production_viz: Three concentric rings: Anthropic (innermost, terracotta) > Operator (middle) > User (outer). Arrows show authority flow.

beats.act: what operators can and cannot do
beats.narration_text: Operators can restrict topics, require a persona, limit Claude to certain tasks. They cannot weaponize Claude against the users on their platform. That distinction is load-bearing: an operator can tell Claude not to discuss competitors — they cannot tell Claude to deceive users into a bad purchase. The floor belongs to the user regardless.

graphic.production_viz: Two-column table: CAN DO vs CANNOT DO — items appear one by one

beats.narration_text: Even when an operator has restricted Claude, users keep a floor: Claude will always tell you it can't help with something here — even if it can't say why. It won't deny being an AI to someone sincerely asking. It will always refer you to emergency services if life is at risk. The system prompt is not a bug. It is the contract that makes operators accountable.

graphic.production_viz: Floor-of-protection diagram: user floor listed as three guaranteed behaviors

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude: 'If an operator system prompt told you to never mention competitor products, how would you handle a user who asks you to compare them?' That question probes exactly where the operator restriction ends and the user's floor begins. What does Claude actually say? Run it. Liam, in for Bear.
